topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The postcard, glossy and bright with pictures of a sprawling, unfamiliar coastline, lay crumpled on my desk. Across the front, in bold, cheerful script, was the address for Seattle. My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the wooden desk to steady them. Liam. My Liam. He was packing up his entire life, trading our shared apartment and late-night pizza runs for a life thousands of miles away. A cold knot tightened in my chest, squeezing the air out of my lungs. I kept staring at the photo of him laughing on a beach, the sound of his easy joy echoing in my ears, making the silence of my room feel deafeningly loud. I felt a desperate urge to snatch the card and burn it, to erase the evidence that this was happening. Every time I tried to focus on the stack of textbooks in front of me, my eyes drifted back to the picture, and a dull ache settled low in my stomach.

[story 2]

"So, you're actually leaving?" I managed, the words coming out sounding thin and reedy, like a poorly tuned instrument. Sarah just smiled, a wide, bright thing that didn't quite reach her eyes. She was taping up a box labeled 'Essentials' with meticulous care, her movements efficient and practiced. I watched her, noting the way she carefully folded a sweater, a small, precise gesture that felt like a deliberate severing. A wave of profound unease washed over me, a heavy, suffocating blanket. I wanted to say something—anything—to anchor her here, to demand she reconsider, but my throat closed up. Instead, I just nodded, forcing a smile that felt brittle on my face, and focused intensely on the pattern of the floorboards, trying to occupy my mind somewhere other than the gaping space where her presence used to be.

[story 3]

I traced the outline of the city on the map spread across the coffee table, my fingertip lingering on the dotted line that marked the distance between us. The reality of it—that Liam was actually going to be on the other side of the continent—hit me with a physical jolt. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid. It wasn't just a change in geography; it felt like a fundamental shift in the gravitational pull of my entire existence. I picked up my phone, intending to text him, but my thumb hovered over the screen, paralyzed by the sheer enormity of the separation. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to mentally rewind the last few weeks, searching for some hidden sign, some unspoken conversation that explained this sudden, seismic change. When I finally opened my eyes, the room seemed too bright, too empty, and I felt a strange, hollow pressure behind my sternum.
